title: Burns
source: American Burn Association / American Red Cross

slides.title: Remove from Heat Source
slides.content: Move the person safely away from the heat source. Remove burned clothing and jewelry near the burn — unless clothing is stuck to the skin. Do not pull stuck fabric.
slides.warning: Do not remove clothing stuck to skin. Do not break blisters.
slides.tts_text: Move away from heat. Remove clothing and jewelry near the burn unless stuck to skin.

slides.title: Cool the Burn
slides.content: Hold the burned area under cool (not cold) running water for 10–20 minutes. This is the single most important step — it reduces depth and pain.
slides.warning: No ice. No ice water. No butter. No toothpaste. No creams. Cool running water only. Stop if person starts shivering uncontrollably.
slides.tts_text: Cool with cool running water for 10 to 20 minutes. No ice. No butter. Cool water only.

slides.title: Assess Burn Depth
slides.content: 1st degree: Red, dry, painful, no blisters (like sunburn). 2nd degree: Blisters, wet/shiny, very painful. 3rd degree: White, brown, or charred, may be painless (nerve damage destroyed). Call 911 for 3rd degree or large 2nd degree burns.
slides.tts_text: Assess the burn. First degree is red and painful. Second degree has blisters. Third degree is white or charred and may not hurt.

slides.title: 1st Degree — Home Care
slides.content: After cooling: apply aloe vera gel or unscented moisturizer. Cover loosely with a non-stick sterile dressing or clean cloth. Take OTC pain relief if needed. Seek medical care if burn is larger than 3 inches or involves face, hands, feet, genitals, or major joints.
slides.tts_text: After cooling, apply aloe or moisturizer. Cover loosely with a sterile dressing.

slides.title: 2nd Degree — Cover and Seek Care
slides.content: After cooling: cover loosely with a sterile non-stick dressing. Do not pop blisters — they protect against infection. Do not apply creams, butter, or ointment. Seek medical care for 2nd degree burns larger than 3 inches, or on face, hands, feet, genitals, or major joints.
slides.warning: Do not pop blisters — they are a natural barrier against infection.
slides.tts_text: Cover loosely with sterile dressing. Do not pop blisters. Seek medical care for larger burns.

slides.title: 3rd Degree — Call 911 Now
slides.content: Call 911. After cooling (if still possible): cover loosely with a cool wet sterile dressing or clean sheet. Do not remove clothing stuck to skin. Keep person warm — 3rd degree burns cause dangerous heat loss. Lay flat and monitor for shock.
slides.warning: 3rd degree burns require emergency surgery. Do not attempt home treatment. All 3rd degree burns = 911.
slides.tts_text: Call 911. Cover loosely with a cool wet sterile dressing. Do not remove stuck clothing. Treat for shock.

slides.title: Next Steps
slides.content: 1st degree: watch for infection signs (spreading redness, pus, fever) over next 48 hours. 2nd/3rd degree: hospital care required. All burns on face, hands, feet, genitals, or joints require physician evaluation regardless of size.

completion.message: Monitor the burn. Seek appropriate medical care based on severity.

slides.tts_text: Monitor for infection. All second and third degree burns need medical care.
